title: NOC 41310 – Police Investigators and Other Investigative Occupations | GTR Immigration
description: Police Investigators and Other Investigative Occupations under NOC 41310 are specialized law enforcement and investigative professionals responsible for conducting criminal investigations, forensic analysis, and death investigations across Canada. Classified as TEER 1 (Professional Occupation), this role supports Canada’s justice system, public safety framework, and regulatory enforcement. This NOC includes police detectives, drug investigators, coroners, and medical examiners working at municipal, provincial, and federal levels.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category4 – Occupations in education, law, and social, community, and government servicesTEER1 – Occupations usually require a university degreeMajor Group41 – Professional occupations in law, education, social, community and government servicesSub-Major Group413 – Professional occupations in social and community servicesMinor Group4131 – Police investigators and probation officersNOC Code41310

duties: Prepare investigation reports and testify in court
Obtain search warrants and make arrests
Determine identity, cause, and manner of death
Order or perform autopsies and medical tests
Consult with law enforcement and medical professionals
Educate the public on safety risks
employment_requirements: Police Investigators
Completion of secondary school
Several years of experience as a police officer
In Quebec, a university degree in police investigation required
Specialized training or certification may be required (forensic, cybercrime, narcotics)
Coroners and Medical Examiners
Requirements vary by province:
In Alberta, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, and Newfoundland & Labrador:Medical examiner must be a licensed physician
Medical examiner must be a licensed physician
In Ontario and Prince Edward Island:Coroner must be a licensed physician
Coroner must be a licensed physician
In Quebec:Must be a non-retired member of:Collège des médecins du QuébecBarreau du QuébecChambre des notaires du QuébecMinimum four years of professional experience
Must be a non-retired member of:Collège des médecins du QuébecBarreau du QuébecChambre des notaires du Québec
Collège des médecins du Québec
Barreau du Québec
Chambre des notaires du Québec
Minimum four years of professional experience
In British Columbia, Saskatchewan, New Brunswick, Northwest Territories, Nunavut, and Yukon:Education and experience in legal, medical, or investigative fields preferred
Education and experience in legal, medical, or investigative fields preferred
Canadian citizenship or permanent residency is typically required for police service employment.
salary_prospects: Police Investigators and related professionals typically earn between:
$55,600 – $111,400 per year, depending on rank, specialization, and province.
Senior investigators and supervisory officers may earn higher compensation.
job_outlook: The outlook for NOC 41310 remains stable and positive due to:
Expansion of forensic and digital investigation units
However, entry into policing roles is highly competitive and regulated.
labour_market_demand: Professionals under NOC 41310 are employed by:
Increased cybercrime and financial crime investigations
Strengthened public safety and regulatory enforcement
Opportunities are available nationwide, particularly in Ontario, Alberta, British Columbia, and Quebec.
Career advancement into supervisory or specialized units
Opportunity to work in cybercrime, homicide, narcotics, and forensic divisions
Positions are highly regulated and competitive.
pathways: While classified as TEER 1, many police investigator roles require:
Express Entry (if eligible under skilled categories)
Career transition into related investigative roles
